description: A central-radiating directed neuron web with ring and cross-links for educational signal-flow visualization.

explainer.what: This neuron is an entry-point sensory relay that injects external drive into the network.
explainer.how: It propagates short-latency excitatory pulses into early integration nodes.
explainer.where: It sits on the input edge of the network in the first layer.
explainer.why: Anchoring propagation here makes it clear how feedforward activity begins and branches.

explainer.what: This neuron is a parallel sensory input channel with matching timing precision.
explainer.how: It drives neighboring relay units to form convergent evidence.
explainer.where: It is placed in the first input row near N01.
explainer.why: Parallel input paths demonstrate how networks combine multiple upstream streams.

explainer.what: This neuron represents a second modality signal entering the same circuit.
explainer.how: It excites selective relay targets with slightly delayed onset.
explainer.where: It occupies the lower input channel in layer zero.
explainer.why: Including a nonvisual input helps show multimodal convergence.

explainer.what: This neuron provides contextual priming input instead of pure feedforward evidence.
explainer.how: Its output scales downstream responsiveness rather than forcing spikes alone.
explainer.where: It sits in the input layer but projects broadly into integration nodes.
explainer.why: Context channels make modulation effects visible and educational.

explainer.what: This neuron is an early relay that pools two upstream streams.
explainer.how: It amplifies synchronized input and forwards it to mid-network hubs.
explainer.where: It is in the second layer on the dorsal side of the network.
explainer.why: It illustrates convergence and threshold-like amplification.

explainer.what: This relay node channels mixed sensory input toward the central path.
explainer.how: It sends medium-weight projections into both excitatory and inhibitory interneurons.
explainer.where: It sits near the geometric center of layer one.
explainer.why: Balanced projections help explain divergent routing.

explainer.what: This neuron acts as a central relay across competing pathways.
explainer.how: Its outputs split toward inhibitory gating and forward projection.
explainer.where: It is positioned on the midline of layer one.
explainer.why: This split clarifies how one node can influence opposite outcomes.

explainer.what: This relay carries lower-path sensory and contextual mixtures.
explainer.how: It provides sustained drive to modulatory and motor-prep nodes.
explainer.where: It appears on the ventral side of layer one.
explainer.why: A lower-path relay makes spatial pathway differences easy to read.

explainer.what: This interneuron provides fast inhibitory gating to prevent runaway spread.
explainer.how: It suppresses selected hubs when incoming activity exceeds moderate levels.
explainer.where: It is in the first integration layer near upper relay nodes.
explainer.why: It shows how inhibition sharpens signal selectivity.

explainer.what: This neuron is a complementary inhibitory gate on the lower branch.
explainer.how: It dampens competing routes and limits late reverberation.
explainer.where: It sits ventrally in layer one.
explainer.why: Paired inhibitory gates make directional suppression visible.

explainer.what: This hub aggregates upper-branch relays into coherent downstream drive.
explainer.how: It integrates excitatory input while receiving inhibitory veto from gate A.
explainer.where: It is located in the central layer toward the dorsal side.
explainer.why: It demonstrates balance between drive and control.

explainer.what: This hub routes mixed sensory evidence toward decision and motor-prep nodes.
explainer.how: Its weighted outgoing edges distribute graded drive based on upstream timing.
explainer.where: It occupies the central corridor of layer two.
explainer.why: Central hubs make branching propagation legible.

explainer.what: This hub couples lower sensory streams into forward relay pathways.
explainer.how: It receives excitation plus inhibitory sculpting from gate B.
explainer.where: It lies in the lower-middle region of layer two.
explainer.why: It clarifies how inhibition can redirect rather than fully block flow.

explainer.what: This node carries lower-path context to output planning units.
explainer.how: It has stronger sensitivity to modulatory transmitter modes.
explainer.where: It is ventral in the central layer.
explainer.why: It demonstrates state-dependent gain in contextual pathways.

explainer.what: This neuron symbolizes a modulatory dopaminergic influence over hubs.
explainer.how: It scales downstream responses instead of acting as binary excitation alone.
explainer.where: It sits above the main hub lane with broad projections.
explainer.why: It helps separate modulation from direct feedforward signaling.

explainer.what: This neuron represents cholinergic contextual enhancement of selected pathways.
explainer.how: It increases transmission gain in task-relevant edges.
explainer.where: It is placed below the main hub corridor in layer two.
explainer.why: It demonstrates selective amplification rather than global excitation.

explainer.what: This late-stage neuron accumulates evidence for one output branch.
explainer.how: It receives convergent drive from hubs and modulatory bias inputs.
explainer.where: It lies in the upper output-prep layer.
explainer.why: It visualizes commitment signals after mid-network integration.

explainer.what: This neuron encodes an alternative decision trajectory.
explainer.how: It competes with neighboring late-stage neurons through shared inhibitory control.
explainer.where: It sits near the center of the late integration layer.
explainer.why: Parallel decision units make competition and bias easy to interpret.

explainer.what: This neuron is a lower-branch decision integrator.
explainer.how: It combines hub input with serotonin-like contextual dampening.
explainer.where: It appears in the lower middle of layer three.
explainer.why: It shows how context can slow but stabilize signal escalation.

explainer.what: This neuron represents ventral route commitment to output channels.
explainer.how: It forwards sustained but slower pulses to execution nodes.
explainer.where: It occupies the ventral end of layer three.
explainer.why: It highlights timing differences across pathway depth.

explainer.what: This neuron models serotonergic contextual regulation of output readiness.
explainer.how: It tempers high-gain transitions and smooths downstream activation.
explainer.where: It sits adjacent to decision nodes with broad modulatory projections.
explainer.why: This makes stabilizing modulation visible in final-stage routing.

explainer.what: This interneuron clamps excessive late-stage spread.
explainer.how: It applies broad inhibitory feedback onto decision and output targets.
explainer.where: It is placed below the decision lane in layer three.
explainer.why: A clamp unit makes global inhibitory regulation visually explicit.

explainer.what: This is a terminal output neuron for the upper execution path.
explainer.how: It sums decision-layer drive and reflects final propagated signal intensity.
explainer.where: It is located at the right edge of the network in the output layer.
explainer.why: Output nodes provide a clear endpoint for educational signal tracing.

explainer.what: This is the terminal output neuron for the lower execution path.
explainer.how: It receives convergent late-stage signals and inhibitory balancing input.
explainer.where: It sits beside N23 at the final network layer.
explainer.why: A paired endpoint makes branch outcomes easy to compare at a glance.
